title.rendered: Step-by-step guide to getting a UAE trade licence

content.rendered:
Choosing the right UAE trade licence route is the difference between a structure that banks welcome and one that gets quietly declined. Entrepreneurs and investors often rush into the cheapest package, then discover later that their licence, activities or free zone choice does not match what UAE banks or foreign tax authorities expect.

A trade licence is the legal permission to carry out specific activities in a specific jurisdiction, under a specific regulator. In the UAE that might mean a Dubai mainland licence from the Department of Economy and Tourism (DET, often still called DED), an Abu Dhabi licence, or a free zone licence from DMCC, IFZA, RAKEZ or others. Each has different rules on ownership, substance, reporting and who you can trade with.

The process is not just a form and a payment. You need to decide shareholding, management, tax residency, office arrangements and banking strategy before you choose a licence. Only then should you move on to name reservation, activity selection, initial approval, document signing, payment and visa steps.

The sections that follow walk through these decisions in order so you can choose the right route and execute a clean, bank-ready application.

excerpt.rendered: Choosing the right UAE trade licence is critical if you want a structure that banks will accept and regulators respect. This guide explains mainland vs free zone options, licence types, realistic costs and timelines, and how to build a bank-ready business from day one.

acf.Key_takeaways:
• A UAE trade licence is issued by bodies such as Dubai DET, Abu Dhabi DED or DMCC and directly affects banking, tax and visa options.
  • Mainland licences in Dubai often cost around AED 12,000 to 20,000 in government fees, while many free zone packages start near AED 10,000.
  • Processing times are typically 3 to 7 working days for straightforward cases in Dubai or DMCC once documents and approvals are in order.
  • Financial services structures may require ADGM or DIFC authorisation on top of a standard licence, bringing FSRA or DFSA into the process.
  • Bank-ready planning before applying for a UAE trade licence helps avoid later account rejections and costly restructuring in Dubai or other emirates.
acf.Keypoint_One: The cheapest or fastest UAE trade licence is rarely the one that UAE banks or foreign tax authorities will accept without questions.
acf.more_content_1:
What a UAE trade licence is

A UAE trade licence is the core document that allows your company to legally conduct specific activities in a particular emirate or free zone. It is issued either by a mainland economic department, such as Dubai DET (formerly DED), or by a free zone authority such as DMCC, IFZA or RAKEZ. The licence type, issuing authority and wording of your activities will directly affect tax exposure, banking options and ownership flexibility.

Mainland vs free zone licences

Mainland licences are issued by the economic departments of each emirate, for example Dubai DET or Abu Dhabi DED. A Dubai mainland DED trade license allows you to trade anywhere in the UAE market and abroad, including directly with UAE consumers and government entities. You can open offices or branches across Dubai without restriction, subject to zoning rules. Free zone trade licences are issued by specific free zone authorities, such as DMCC in JLT, IFZA in Dubai, or RAKEZ in Ras Al Khaimah. These licences are designed primarily for trading within the free zone and internationally. Direct onshore trading with UAE customers usually requires a local distributor, a mainland branch or special approvals. Cost structures also differ. A straightforward Dubai mainland commercial licence with a simple office can start around AED 12,000 to 18,000 in government fees, excluding office rent. Many free zone packages advertise entry-level licences from roughly AED 10,000 to 15,000, often including a flexi-desk. However, lower free zone fees can be offset later by banking challenges or substance requirements in your home country.

Key regulators and authorities

The main licensing bodies you will encounter include:
  • Dubai DET (DED) for Dubai mainland licences.
  • Abu Dhabi DED for Abu Dhabi mainland licences.
  • DMCC for commodity trading, crypto and service businesses in JLT.
  • IFZA for cost-effective service and trading structures in Dubai.
  • RAKEZ for industrial, logistics and holding structures in Ras Al Khaimah.
Each authority has its own activity list, documentation standards and processing timelines. A straightforward Dubai mainland licence can often be issued in 3 to 7 working days after documents are in order. A DMCC or IFZA licence can be as fast as 3 to 5 working days once KYC is cleared. These choices also interact with banking and tax. For example, some UAE and international banks are more comfortable with well-known regulators such as DMCC or Dubai mainland for trading firms. Where 100% foreign ownership in Dubai is available, it must still be paired with activities and substance that make sense to compliance teams.
acf.more_content_2:
Choosing the right licence type

Once you understand where to license, you must decide what type of UAE business license fits your model. The main categories are commercial, professional, industrial and, in practice, e-commerce variants of these. The wrong choice can block visas, delay bank account opening or require unexpected external approvals.

Commercial, professional and industrial

Commercial licences cover trading in goods and many general services. Examples include general trading, foodstuffs, electronics, wholesale and retail activities. In Dubai mainland, some commercial activities still require additional approvals from bodies such as Dubai Municipality or the Ministry of Economy. Professional licences apply to service providers where the key asset is expertise. This includes consulting, IT services, marketing, design and some healthcare and education services. Regulators may require degrees, professional qualifications or experience letters for certain activities, especially in Abu Dhabi and Dubai. Industrial licences are for manufacturing, processing and assembly. These usually require a physical industrial facility, environmental approvals and sometimes federal-level clearances. Fees are higher and timelines longer because of site inspections and utility requirements.
  • A simple Dubai commercial licence for trading can have government fees around AED 12,000 to 20,000, excluding office rent.
  • A professional licence in a free zone such as IFZA can start near AED 10,000 including a flexi-desk and up to 3 visa quotas.
  • Industrial licences in RAKEZ or Abu Dhabi can involve total first-year costs of AED 80,000+ once you include warehouse rent and utilities.
  • Some regulated activities, such as financial services, require separate approvals from the Central Bank, ESCA or free zone financial regulators.

Mainland, free zone or ADGM DIFC

For most trading and service businesses the choice is between mainland and a standard free zone trade licence. Mainland offers easier access to the UAE market and government tenders. Free zones offer simplified set-up, often lower minimum capital and more flexible office options. However, if you are building an investment firm, asset manager, fintech or financial advisory business, you should also consider ADGM in Abu Dhabi and DIFC in Dubai. These are financial free zones with their own common law frameworks and regulators, the FSRA in ADGM and DFSA in DIFC. For example, a wealth manager might use an ADGM Cat 4 licence for advisory services while holding operating entities in DMCC or mainland. Choosing between Dubai, Abu Dhabi and other emirates depends on your client base, visa needs, physical presence and long-term exit plans. A holding company for European assets might sit in RAKEZ or Dubai, while a regulated fintech might combine DIFC with a separate Dubai trade license for local operations.
acf.quote_1: Decide your activities and regulators first, then choose the cheapest viable package, not the other way around.
acf.more_content_3:
Step-by-step UAE trade licence process

The process to obtain a UAE trade licence is predictable if you sequence decisions correctly. For a typical entrepreneur or family office, the key is to lock in structure and tax assumptions before you press submit on any application.

Pre-licensing structuring decisions

Start with shareholding and control. Many activities now allow 100% foreign ownership Dubai on the mainland, but some still require a local partner or a local service agent for professional licences. You need to decide who will be shareholder, who will be manager, and where those individuals are tax resident. Consider whether you need a holding company above the UAE entity, for example in Cyprus or the UK, and whether you want multiple UAE entities for risk separation. Think about where directors will live, how many visas you need, and whether you will maintain real substance in the UAE, such as an office and employees. At this stage a client profile might look like this: a European trading firm wants a Dubai mainland licence to sell to UAE customers and GCC distributors, with the owner remaining tax resident in Europe. We would typically recommend a Dubai mainland commercial licence, real office space and a clear board structure that banks can recognise.

Application, approvals and timelines

Once structure is agreed, the operational steps are:
  • Activity selection and name reservation. Choose exact activities from the authority list and reserve a trade name. This usually takes 1 to 2 working days.
  • Initial approval. Submit passport copies, shareholder details, and a simple business plan where required. For most mainland and free zone licences this is 2 to 5 working days.
  • Documentation and payment. Sign the MOA or shareholder resolution, secure a lease or flexi-desk agreement, then pay government fees. A standard Dubai trade license might involve AED 12,000 to 20,000 in government charges.
  • Licence issuance, visas and banking. Once the licence is issued, apply for establishment card, visas and Emirates IDs, then prepare a bank file with KYC, contracts and proof of substance.
Free zone licences such as DMCC or IFZA can often be completed within 1 to 2 weeks from document readiness. Mainland licences can be similar, but office search, notarisation and external approvals can extend this to 3 to 4 weeks. The quality of your initial structuring work will heavily influence how smoothly banking proceeds.
acf.more_content_4:
Aston VIP's role in your UAE trade licence

Aston VIP works with entrepreneurs, trading firms, fintechs and family offices that need bank-ready, internationally credible UAE structures, not just a piece of paper on a wall. Our role is to design the structure, coordinate with regulators and banks, and ensure the entity can actually be used from day one.

Designing a bank-ready structure

We start with your commercial model and tax footprint, then design a shareholding and governance structure that fits both UAE rules and foreign tax expectations. That may mean combining a Dubai mainland entity with a holding company elsewhere, or using a specific free zone for certain activities and another for holding assets. For banking, we align activities, expected turnover, counterparties and substance with the risk appetite of UAE banks. That includes advising on realistic office choices, director residency, signatory mix and documentation. For example, a proprietary trading firm will face different questions from a logistics company or a consulting practice, even if their legal licences appear similar.

Managing regulators and free zones

Aston VIP deals directly with economic departments, free zone authorities and, where relevant, financial regulators. For a simple commercial licence this might involve clarifying activity wording with Dubai DET or DMCC so that it fits both your model and bank compliance expectations. For more complex structures, such as fintechs or investment managers, we coordinate between ADGM or DIFC regulators and your onshore entities. Our team understands how each authority interprets business plans, substance and control, which reduces the risk of late-stage objections or reclassification of your activities.

Ongoing compliance and accounting

Once the licence is issued, the real work begins. Aston VIP supports clients with corporate governance, ESR analysis, VAT registration where thresholds are met, and coordination of annual audits where required by the free zone or bank. We also work with specialist partners for accounting and bookkeeping services so that your records match what banks and regulators expect to see during periodic reviews. This reduces the risk of account closures or licence non-renewal because of missing filings or weak documentation. To discuss your situation, speak with our team.
acf.quote_2: A bank-ready UAE structure is designed around your real transactions, not around the cheapest advertised licence package.
acf.more_content_5:
How long does it take to get a UAE trade licence?

For a straightforward case with documents ready, many Dubai mainland and free zone licences can be issued in 3 to 7 working days after initial approval. If external approvals, notarisation or industrial facilities are involved, the process can extend to 3 to 4 weeks. Banking, visas and Emirates IDs usually add several weeks on top of licence issuance.

Can I get a UAE trade licence with 100% foreign ownership in Dubai?

Yes, many activities now allow 100% foreign ownership in Dubai on the mainland, in addition to long-standing full ownership in free zones. However, some strategic sectors still require a UAE partner or special approvals. You must check the specific activity code and emirate rules before assuming full foreign ownership is available.

What is the difference between a Dubai mainland trade license and a free zone licence?

A Dubai mainland trade license from Dubai DET allows you to trade directly with the onshore UAE market and government entities, subject to your activities. A free zone licence, such as DMCC or IFZA, is primarily intended for business within the free zone and international trade, and often requires a local distributor or mainland structure for direct UAE consumer sales. Banking, office requirements and reporting obligations can also differ between mainland and free zones.

Do I need a physical office to get a UAE business license?

Most mainland licences require at least a physical office or dedicated desk, evidenced by a lease and Ejari or Tawtheeq registration. Many free zones allow flexi-desk or shared office solutions that meet minimum substance and visa requirements. For industrial or regulated activities, a proper facility is usually mandatory and may be inspected before or after licence issuance.
acf.Keypoint_Two: The quality of your pre-licensing structuring decisions will matter far more to banks than how quickly your trade licence was printed.
acf.more_content_6:
Costs, timelines and common mistakes

Realistic fees and timeframes

For a standard Dubai trade license on the mainland, expect government fees in the region of AED 12,000 to 20,000 for a basic commercial or professional licence, excluding office rent and professional advisory costs. Free zones such as IFZA or RAKEZ often advertise packages from around AED 10,000 to 15,000 including a flexi-desk and a small visa quota. Professional advisory fees vary widely, but for a properly structured engagement that includes tax and banking input, many international clients budget USD 5,000 to 15,000, depending on complexity. Simple service firms will sit at the lower end, while multi-entity groups or regulated businesses will be higher. Licensing timelines are usually 1 to 2 weeks for straightforward free zone applications and 2 to 4 weeks for mainland structures, especially where office search, notarisation or external approvals are required. Banking can then take anywhere from 1 to 8 weeks, depending on the bank, your profile and the quality of your documentation.

Avoiding structural and banking errors

The most expensive mistakes are usually made before the first form is submitted. Common issues include choosing a free zone trade licence that does not match your real trading pattern, selecting activities that are too broad or too narrow, or using nominee shareholders without understanding foreign tax implications. Banking problems often stem from weak substance, such as no real office, no staff, or a business plan that does not match the licence activities. Some clients also discover that their chosen free zone is less familiar to major banks, which leads to extended compliance reviews or quiet declines. Another frequent error is ignoring home-country tax rules. A structure that looks efficient in the UAE can trigger controlled foreign company or management-and-control issues elsewhere. Addressing these points at the structuring stage is far cheaper than unwinding a group after the first tax enquiry or bank review.
acf.Final_Points:
• Map your real business model, counterparties and target markets before deciding on mainland, free zone or financial free zone options.
  • List the exact activities you need and cross-check them against authority lists in Dubai, Abu Dhabi, DMCC, IFZA or RAKEZ.
  • Budget realistically for government fees, office costs, advisory input and at least 1 to 2 months for licensing and banking.
  • Engage a specialist advisory firm early to design a bank-ready, tax-aware structure rather than retrofitting compliance later.
